stories.headline: IRAN REOPENS STRAIT OF HORMUZ; TRUMP SAYS WAR 'SHOULD BE ENDING PRETTY SOON'
stories.body: Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi declared the Strait fully open to commercial traffic for the duration of the 10-day Israel-Lebanon ceasefire. About 20% of global oil and 20%+ of LNG transit the chokepoint, so the reopening unwinds a risk premium that had pushed Brent to $115 in late March and March headline CPI to +0.9% MoM.

stories.headline: OIL CRATERS 10-12% AS SUPPLY FEARS EVAPORATE
stories.body: WTI May futures fell ~12% to $83.20 and Brent June contract dropped ~10.5% to $88.96 — the single-day move that fully retraces the Iran-war spike. The plunge will pull roughly 0.3-0.5pp off headline CPI over the next 1-2 prints and immediately compresses energy-sector earnings estimates into Q1 reports.

stories.headline: S&P 500 NOTCHES FRESH RECORD CLOSE; RUSSELL 2000 LEADS ON PEACE HOPES
stories.body: S&P 500 +0.62% to ~7,148, Nasdaq +0.95%, Dow +1.0%, Russell 2000 +0.22%. Small caps and airlines led as lower oil and fading geopolitical tail risk improved the domestic-cyclical trade. 10Y yield slipped ~5 bps to 4.17%; gold still up 1.65% to $4,867 on residual hedging demand; DXY -0.52% to 97.70.

stories.headline: FED PATH REPRICED AHEAD OF APRIL 28-29 FOMC
stories.body: With oil reversing and March CPI at 3.3% YoY (driven almost entirely by a 21.2% gasoline surge), futures are edging back toward at least one 2026 cut after Wells Fargo and others had shifted to zero. Fed funds stand at 3.50-3.75%; March dot plot showed a median of one more cut this year and three in 2027. Next week's meeting is the tell.

stories.headline: TRANSFER PORTAL HEATS UP AS APRIL 21 DEADLINE APPROACHES
stories.body: Hofstra's Cruz Davis (20.1 PPG, 4.7 APG) commits to Texas Tech; ex-Providence guard Stefan Vaaks picks Illinois; UNC lands former Virginia Tech star Neoklis Avdalas. Portal closes April 21, compressing high-stakes decisions into the next four days. Washington remains a net loser this cycle — six players out including Zoom Diallo to Kentucky.

stories.headline: PLAY-IN CONCLUDES FRIDAY; PLAYOFFS OPEN SATURDAY
stories.body: Friday's play-in matchups settle the 7th and 8th seeds in both conferences. Round 1 tips April 18. The Pistons, as East No. 1 seed, host Game 1 Sunday at 6:30 PM ET on NBC/Peacock against the winner of the Magic-Hornets play-in game.

stories.headline: DRAFT WEEK: MENDOZA PROJECTED NO. 1 OVERALL, JETS HOLD PICKS 2 AND 16
stories.body: The 2026 NFL Draft opens April 23 in Pittsburgh. Collaborative mock drafts have Indiana QB Fernando Mendoza going No. 1; the Jets are tied to edge David Bailey at No. 2 and a WR at 16. Cowboys, Rams, and Jaguars are flagged as the three most likely trade-up targets.

stories.headline: LOUISVILLE SPRING GAME TONIGHT; COACH BROHM LOSES TWO WRS FOR 2026
stories.body: Louisville's spring game kicks off at 6 PM ET (free admission) as the Cards wrap year four under Jeff Brohm. Less welcome: WRs Kris Hughes and Montavin Quisenberry both suffered lower-body injuries in practice and are expected to miss the entire 2026 season. SMU also holds its spring finale tonight.

sports.detail: East No. 1 seed. Game 1 Sunday 4/19, 6:30 PM ET vs. Magic-Hornets play-in winner (NBC/Peacock).
Lost at Padres 5-2 on 4/16. Now 8-12, 1-8 on the road. Vedder Cup series wraps in San Diego.
Four picks starting at No. 32. Top needs: RB, edge, CB. Hosted DE Dante Fowler Jr. at Renton this week.
ESPN way-too-early bumped UW to No. 17. Transfer WR Bodpegn Miller (Ohio St.) standing out. Dawgs After Dark open practice tonight; Spring Game May 1.
Freshman Hannes Steinbach to NBA Draft; Diallo (Kentucky) and Kepnang among six portal departures. Coach Sprinkle adds Steele Venters (8th college season) and is hosting LeJuan Watts.

watchlist: FOMC meeting April 28-29 — rate decision + updated statement
NFL Draft Round 1, Thursday April 23 in Pittsburgh (ESPN/ABC)
College basketball transfer portal closes Tuesday April 21
NBA Playoffs Game 1: Pistons host Sunday April 19, 6:30 PM ET
Mariners at Angels series, Friday-Sunday (next homestand starts Monday)

deepDive.title: Oil's pass-through to CPI — why today's 12% crude crash matters for the April 28 Fed meeting
content: Today's collapse in crude — WTI -12% to $83, Brent -10.5% to $89 — directly reverses the spike that has driven nearly every hot inflation datapoint of the past six weeks. March headline CPI printed +0.9% MoM (3.3% YoY), a scary number on the surface, but the BLS decomposition was unambiguous: energy +10.9%, gasoline +21.2%, and that single category accounted for roughly three-quarters of the monthly all-items gain. Markets have spent April repricing the Fed path from 'two cuts' to 'zero cuts' on the basis of that print. A single-session oil reversal of this size re-opens that debate immediately.

The pass-through works through three channels on three timelines. First, the direct channel: gasoline has a ~3.2% weight in headline CPI and tracks wholesale unleaded with a two-to-six week lag, so a sustained $10/bbl decline in WTI translates mechanically to roughly 0.2-0.3pp off headline CPI within one to two monthly prints. Second, the indirect channel: oil is an input into petrochemicals, plastics, freight (diesel), and air travel, which filters into core goods and some services over three to six months. The Fed staff rule of thumb: a $10/bbl sustained move shifts headline CPI by ~0.4pp and core by ~0.05-0.10pp over 12 months. Third, expectations: 5y5y breakevens typically track front-month crude with roughly a 0.5 beta in episodes like this.

The historical analogue most traders are running in their heads is 2022-2023. After Russia invaded Ukraine, Brent spiked from $95 to $127 in three weeks; headline CPI peaked at 9.1% that June. When crude round-tripped back near $75 by year-end, headline fell to 6.5% — a ~2.6pp decline — but core CPI, which excludes food and energy, only fell from 5.9% to 5.7% over the same window. That's the uncomfortable lesson: oil giveth and oil taketh away on headline, but it leaves core services essentially untouched. The sticky components — shelter, medical services, insurance, labor-intensive services — are a wage story, not an oil story, and those are what the Fed actually targets.

So what does today's move tell you about April 28-29? If WTI holds near $83 through month-end, April CPI (released May 13) will almost certainly print below consensus on headline, and the energy contribution to YoY inflation will flip from tailwind to headwind by summer. That gives Powell meaningful top-cover to retain the 'one cut this year' median from the March dot plot — or even restore optionality for two. But the core-services story has not changed: shelter disinflation has stalled near 4.5% YoY, wage growth is still running hot, and services ex-housing has been sticky around 4% annualized. Expect a hawkish-hold with softer language on energy-driven price pressures. The 2Y at 3.78% — down only 1 bp today versus the 10Y's 5 bp drop — is already telling you the front end does not believe the disinflation is structural yet.
